items.article_h1: Fireworks AI Launches Training Platform in Preview for Frontier Model Development
items.article_standfirst: Fireworks AI has released its training platform in preview, enabling teams to train and deploy frontier AI models end-to-end on a single unified system.
article_body: Fireworks AI has announced the preview release of Fireworks Training, a new platform designed to let organizations train and deploy frontier AI models from a single, unified environment. The announcement was made via a banner on the company's homepage, with the full product described as an end-to-end solution offering three distinct surfaces tailored to different team structures.

The platform targets organizations looking to bring their model development and deployment workflows under one roof, rather than stitching together separate training and inference infrastructure. Fireworks AI has not yet disclosed detailed technical specifications, pricing tiers, or the scope of model sizes the platform supports.

Alongside the new training product, the company's blog also hosts recent technical articles covering topics such as scaling frontier model training, reinforcement learning costs, and training-inference numerical consistency in Mixture of Experts (MoE) models.
article_why_matters: The AI infrastructure market has been dominated by separate tools for training and serving models; a unified platform could simplify workflows for teams building frontier-level models.
The addition of a training capability positions Fireworks AI as a more complete competitor to providers like AWS, Google Cloud, and CoreWeave in the AI cloud space.

Organizations looking to reduce vendor fragmentation in their AI stack may find Fireworks Training an attractive option, especially if it offers streamlined integration with Fireworks' existing inference APIs.
article_source_note: The announcement appears on the Fireworks AI homepage banner and the preview product page. Specific feature details, supported model scales, and availability timelines have not yet been published — teams interested in access should visit fireworks.ai/train directly.

items.article_h1: llama.cpp Patch Release b8925 Fixes Structured Output Parser Bug
items.article_standfirst: The llama.cpp project shipped a quick bug-fix release on April 24, 2026, addressing a critical flaw in its structured output parser.
article_body: The ggml-org/llama.cpp repository published release b8925 on Thursday, April 24, 2026, resolving a structured output bug reported as issue #22302. The fix is straightforward — the release notes describe it simply as "fix very stupid structured output bug" — but the issue had enough impact to warrant its own point release outside the normal cadence.

Alongside the parser fix, this release continues llama.cpp's tradition of distributing prebuilt binaries across a vast range of platforms. Available builds span macOS (Apple Silicon with optional KleidiAI acceleration, and Intel x64), iOS as an XCFramework, Linux across multiple CPU architectures (x64, arm64, s390x) with backend options including Vulkan, ROCm 7.2, OpenVINO, and SYCL in both FP32 and FP16 variants, Android arm64, Windows in x64 and arm64 with CPU, CUDA 12 and 13, Vulkan, SYCL, and HIP backends, and even openEuler x86.

Structured output — the ability to force LLM inference to emit JSON or other machine-readable formats — is a frequently used feature in AI tooling. A parser-level bug in that path could silently corrupt output or cause inference loops, making a targeted patch like this one high priority for developers relying on llama.cpp for production applications.
article_why_matters: Structured output bugs can cause silent failures in AI pipelines, producing malformed JSON that downstream code cannot parse.
The breadth of platforms covered by llama.cpp's binary distribution means this fix impacts a wide ecosystem of desktop, mobile, server, and edge deployments.
Developers using llama.cpp for JSON-constrained inference in agents, tools, or RAG pipelines will want to update immediately to avoid corrupted outputs.
article_source_note: This article is based on the official llama.cpp GitHub release page at https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/releases/tag/b8925, which was published on April 24, 2026 at 23:41 UTC. The release contains only one code change — the structured output parser fix (issue #22302) — alongside updated prebuilt binaries for all supported platforms.

items.article_h1: GLM-5.1 Agentic Engineering Model Launches on DeepInfra Cloud Platform
items.article_standfirst: DeepInfra has added GLM-5.1, described as a state-of-the-art agentic engineering model, to its cloud inference catalogue.
article_body: DeepInfra has quietly added GLM-5.1 to its model catalogue, marketing it as a state-of-the-art agentic engineering model. The addition positions the platform as a one-stop hub for a growing range of frontier and open-weight models spanning families including Claude, DeepSeek, Llama, Mistral, Qwen, Flux, Gemini, and Nemotron. DeepInfra's pricing model remains straightforward: a pay-what-you-use structure with no long-term contracts and no upfront costs required. The platform currently lists models across categories such as automatic speech recognition, embeddings, reranking, text generation, text-to-image, text-to-speech, and text-to-video.
article_why_matters: Agentic engineering models are designed to autonomously plan and execute multi-step tasks, making them increasingly relevant for developers building autonomous AI agents.
DeepInfra's no-commitment, pay-as-you-go model lowers the barrier for developers and organisations to experiment with frontier models at scale.
The addition of GLM-5.1 expands the range of agentic models available alongside existing open-weight families, giving developers more deployment options.
article_source_note: The information comes from DeepInfra's pricing page (deepinfra.com/pricing). The page displays model availability and pricing philosophy but does not include per-model pricing tiers or technical benchmarks for GLM-5.1 at this time.

items.article_h1: DeepSeek-V4 Enters Hugging Face 'Hot' Feed, Highlighting Million-Token Context for Agents
items.article_standfirst: DeepSeek-V4, a model supporting a one-million-token context window, has appeared in the Hot articles section of the Hugging Face community blog.
article_body: DeepSeek-V4 has appeared in the "Hot" articles section of the Hugging Face community blog, marking one of the first times the model has surfaced in the platform's curated editorial feed. The article, dated April 24, 2026, frames the model as built specifically for agentic use cases — applications where an AI must reason across very long, multi-step conversations or documents.

The key highlight is the model's one-million-token context window. In practical terms, this allows an agent to ingest and reason across an entire large codebase, a multi-hour transcript, or a full legal docket without losing earlier context. The article claims this is not just a raw benchmark achievement but something agents can actually exploit in production workflows.

The entry follows recent community discussion on long-context reasoning and sparse mixture-of-experts architectures, both areas where DeepSeek has invested heavily across its model family. It landed on the Hot feed alongside notable articles on Gemma 4's on-device multimodal capabilities and an explainer on KV caching optimization.
article_why_matters: A million-token context enables agents to process entire codebases, document sets, or multi-session conversations without fragmenting tasks — a practical step toward truly autonomous AI.
DeepSeek has consistently released models with strong open-weight availability, meaning this capability could be accessible to self-hosted and fine-tuning researchers at low cost.
Its placement on the Hugging Face Hot feed signals the community is actively engaging with long-context agentic design as a priority, not just a benchmark curiosity.
article_source_note: The article was captured from the Hugging Face community blog at https://huggingface.co/blog on April 24–25, 2026, where it appeared in the Hot section of the community articles feed. The page is a list of community-submitted articles and does not contain the full DeepSeek-V4 technical write-up — additional detail would require visiting the linked article directly.

items.headline_en: Codex release feed now shows rust-v0.126.0-alpha.1
items.summary_en: The monitored release feed for Codex changed and the newest visible tag is rust-v0.126.0-alpha.1. The feed timestamp is 2026-04-24T22:30:09Z. This looks like a release-level update rather than a cosmetic page edit.
diff_excerpt: content=New Features Added a built-in amazon-bedrock model provider with configurable AWS profile support ( #18744 ). Added /mcp verbose for full MCP server diagnostics, resources, and resource templates while keeping plain /mcp fast ( #18610 ). Made plugin MCP loading accept both mcpServers and top-level server maps in .mcp.json ( #18780 ). Improved realtime handoffs so background agents receive transcript deltas and can explicitly stay silent when appropriate ( #18597 , #18761 , #18635 ). Added host-specific remote_sandbox_config requirements for remote environments ( #18763 ). Refreshed bundled model metadata, including the current gpt-5.4 default ( #18586 , #18388 , #18719 ). Bug Fixes Fixed /copy after rollback so it copies the latest visible assistant response, not a pre-rollback response ( #18739 ). Queued normal follow-up text submitted while a manual shell command is running, preventing stuck Working states ( #18820 ). Fixed Unicode/dead-key input in VS Code WSL terminals by disabling the enhanced keyboard mode there ( #18741 ). Prevented stale proxy environment variables from being restored from shell snapshots ( #17271 ). Made codex exec inherit root-level shared flags such as sandbox and model options ( #18630 ). Removed leaked review prompts from TUI transcripts ( #18659 ). Documentation Added and tightened the Code Review skill instructions used by Codex-driven reviews ( #18746 , #18818 ). Documented intentional await-across-lock cases and enabled Clippy linting for them ( #18423 , #18698 ). Updated app-server protocol docs for threadless MCP resource reads and namespaced dynamic tools ( #18292 , #18413 ). Chores Fixed high-severity dependency alerts by pinning patched JS and Rust dependencies ( #18167 ). Reduced Rust dev build debug-info overhead while preserving useful backtraces ( #18844 ). Refreshed generated Python app-server SDK types from the current schema ( #18862 ). items.article_h1: Portkey Publishes Platform Team Guide to Managing Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex at Scale
items.article_standfirst: Portkey has released a practical guide aimed at platform engineering teams responsible for governing multiple AI coding agents across an engineering organization.
article_body: Portkey, an AI infrastructure company, has published a new guide addressing how platform teams can own and manage AI coding agents — including Anthropic's Claude Code, Cursor, and OpenAI's Codex — across their engineering organization.

The guide, titled 'Who owns Claude Code at your company? A platform team's guide to managing coding agents at scale,' was authored by Siddharth Sambharia and published on April 24, 2026. The piece carries the tagline 'The harness is converging. Context is what separates teams,' suggesting the guide focuses on governance, context management, and standardization as organizations scale agent usage.

The release is part of Portkey's broader 'Production Guides' content track, alongside recent posts covering Skills Registry, OpenAI Codex best practices, AI Agent governance, and an introduction to the Agent Gateway — indicating a coordinated editorial focus on enterprise AI agent operations.
article_why_matters: As AI coding agents proliferate across engineering teams, questions of ownership, governance, and standardization are becoming acute; this guide directly addresses who inside an organization should own that responsibility.
The simultaneous publication of related pieces on Skills Registry, Agent Gateway, and AI Agent governance suggests Portkey is building out a cohesive framework for enterprise AI agent management.

Engineering leaders deploying multiple agents — Claude Code, Cursor, Codex — in parallel will need structured guidance on integration, access control, and observability to avoid fragmented, ungoverned adoption.
article_source_note: This article is based on the Portkey Blog listing page for April 24, 2026. The guide's full content was not retrieved, so this story is limited to the post's title, author, date, excerpt, and tag metadata. Readers should consult the full post at portkey.ai/blog/platfrom-guide-to-coding-agents/ for the complete analysis and recommendations.

items.article_h1: Together AI Expands Serverless Inference Catalog with New Chinese Frontier Models
items.article_standfirst: Together AI has updated its serverless inference pricing to include latest versions of leading Chinese AI models including MiniMax M2.7, Kimi K2.6, and GLM-5.1 alongside established open-weight options like Gemma 4 and Qwen3.5.
article_body:
Together AI has refreshed its serverless inference pricing page with updated model listings and new price points. The update includes the latest iterations of high-profile Chinese models: MiniMax M2.7 now priced at $0.30 per million input tokens (with cached hits at $0.06) and $1.20 for output; Kimi K2.6 at $1.20 input and $4.50 output; and GLM-5.1 at $1.40 input and $4.40 output. Batch API pricing is also listed, offering significantly reduced rates for asynchronous workloads.

The platform continues to offer a broad range of models spanning chat, vision, image, audio, transcription, embeddings, reranking, and moderation categories. Open-weight models like Gemma 4 31B remain competitively priced at $0.20 input and $0.50 output per million tokens, while the recently listed gpt-oss-120B is priced at $0.15 input and $0.60 output. The LFM2 24B A2B model appears as one of the lowest-cost options on the board at $0.03 input per million tokens.

Together AI positions serverless inference as the entry point for most teams, with a migration path to dedicated endpoints as usage scales. The pricing page notes that most teams start serverless before moving to dedicated infrastructure at higher volumes.
article_why_matters: Chinese frontier models like Kimi K2.6 and GLM-5.1 have gained significant traction globally, and their availability on Together AI gives developers a managed serverless option without self-hosting overhead.

The $0.03 per million tokens price on LFM2 24B A2B represents one of the most aggressive pricing points in the current serverless inference market, potentially making large-scale batch processing economically viable for more teams.

The continued expansion of Together AI's model catalog reinforces the platform's strategy as a broad aggregator of inference providers, competing directly with Azure AI Foundry, AWS Bedrock, and Fireworks AI on model variety and pricing transparency.
article_source_note: All pricing data is sourced directly from Together AI's public pricing page (together.ai/pricing) as captured on April 23–24, 2026. Prices are subject to change; batch API rates apply to asynchronous processing and may require separate account configuration. Several model entries (e.g., Qwen3.5 variants) were truncated in the source capture and complete pricing was not available.

items.article_h1: OpenAI API Changelog Refreshed; GPT-Image-1 Entry Appears at Top
items.article_standfirst: OpenAI's public API changelog page now surfaces a new top entry under the GPT-Image-1 heading, signalling a recent update to its image generation capabilities.
article_body: OpenAI has updated its developer-facing API changelog page, placing a new entry at the top of the feed bearing the title 'OpenAI API changelog: gpt-image-1'. The change was captured during a routine page inspection of the platform's changelog at platform.openai.com/docs/changelog. The entry appears alongside a refreshed listing of available models in the documentation, including the latest frontier models such as GPT-5.4, GPT-5.4 mini, and GPT-5.4 nano. The full substance of the changelog entry — what specific API changes, parameter additions, or behaviour updates are included — is not visible in the captured excerpt, leaving the precise nature of the update to be confirmed directly via the page or official channels.
article_why_matters: Developers using OpenAI's image generation APIs need to track updates to avoid unexpected behaviour changes in production.
The presence of a new top-level changelog entry suggests at least one active, recently committed change to the image API surface.
Confirmation of what exactly changed helps teams plan migration or testing cycles for image-related workflows.
article_source_note: The source for this item is the publicly accessible OpenAI API changelog page at platform.openai.com/docs/changelog, captured as part of an automated monitoring pass. The excerpt contains navigation text and model listings; the gpt-image-1 entry is visible as the latest changelog title, but no body copy or version-diff details were included in the capture. Developers should refer directly to the live changelog page for the full update text.

items.headline_en: AWS Security Agent and DevOps Agent Exit Preview, Enter General Availability
items.summary_en: Amazon has made its autonomous frontier agents — the AWS Security Agent for on-demand penetration testing and the AWS DevOps Agent — generally available, signaling a major step toward AI-driven cloud security and operations at scale.
items.evidence_excerpt: Artificial Intelligence AWS launches frontier agents for security testing and cloud operations by Swami Sivasubramanian on 31 MAR 2026 in Announcements , Artificial Intelligence , Security & Governance , Thought Leadership Permalink Comments Share I’m excited to announce that AWS Security Agent on-demand penetration testing and AWS DevOps Agent are now generally available, representing a new class of AI capabilities we announced at re:Invent called frontier agents. These autonomous systems work independently to achieve goals, scale massively to tackle concurrent tasks, and run persistently for hours or days without constant human oversight. Together, these agents are changing the way we secure and operate software. In preview, customers and partners report that AWS Security Agent compre...
diff_excerpt: -Artificial Intelligence AWS launches frontier agents for security testing and cloud operations by Swami Sivasubramanian on 31 MAR 2026 in Announcements , Artificial Intelligence , Security & Governance , Thought Leadership Permalink Comments Share I’m excited to announce that AWS Security Agent on-demand penetration testing and AWS DevOps Agent are now generally available, representing a new class of AI capabilities we announced at re:Invent called frontier agents. These autonomous systems work independently to achieve goals, scale massively to tackle concurrent tasks, and run persistently for hours or days without constant human oversight. Together, these agents are changing the way we secure and operate software. In preview, customers and partners report that AWS Security Agent compresses penetration testing timelines from weeks to hours and the AWS DevOps Agent supports 3–5x faster incident resolution. Amazon Quick for marketing: From scattered data to strategic action by Zach Conley on 23 APR 2026 in Amazon Quick Suite , Artificial Intelligence Permalink Comments Share Amazon Quick changes how you work. You can set it up in minutes and by the end of the day, you will wonder how you ever worked without it. Quick connects with your applications, tools, and data, creating a personal knowledge graph that learns your priorities, preferences, and network. Applying multimodal biological foundation models across therapeutics and patient care by Kristin Ambrosini , Brian Loyal , Zheng Yang , and Mike Tarselli on 23 APR 2026 in Foundation models , Generative AI , Healthcare , Life Sciences Permalink Comments Share In this post, we’ll explore how multimodal BioFMs work, showcase real-world applications in drug discovery and clinical development, and contextualize how AWS enables organizations to build and deploy multimodal BioFMs. Cost-effective multilingual audio transcription at scale with Parakeet-TDT and AWS Batch by Gleb Geinke , Brian Maguire , Justin Leto , and Yusong Wang on 22 APR 2026 in AWS Batch , Compute , High Performance Computing , Technical How-to Permalink Comments Share In this post, we walk through building a scalable, event-driven transcription pipeline that automatically processes audio files uploaded to Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3), and show you how to use Amazon EC2 Spot Instances and buffered streaming inference to further reduce costs. Amazon SageMaker AI now supports optimized generative AI inference recommendations by Mona Mona , Dmitry Soldatkin , Lokeshwaran Ravi , Kareem Syed-Mohammed , and Vinay Arora on 22 APR 2026 in Amazon SageMaker , Amazon SageMaker AI , Generative AI Permalink Comments Share Today, Amazon SageMaker AI supports optimized generative AI inference recommendations. By delivering validated, optimal deployment configurations with performance metrics, Amazon SageMaker AI keeps your model developers focused on building accurate models, not managing infrastructure. Get to your first working agent in minutes: Announcing new features in Amazon Bedrock AgentCore by Madhu Parthasarathy on 22 APR 2026 in Amazon Bedrock , Amazon Bedrock AgentCore , Announcements , Artificial Intelligence Permalink Comments Share Today, we’re introducing new capabilities that further streamline the agent building experience, removing the infrastructure barriers that slow teams down at every stage of agent development from the first prototype through production deployment. Company-wise memory in Amazon Bedrock with Amazon Neptune and Mem0 by Shawn Tsai , Ray Wang , and Zhihao Lin on 22 APR 2026 in Amazon Bedrock , Amazon Neptune , Artificial Intelligence Permalink Comments Share Company-wise memory in Amazon Bedrock, powered by Amazon Neptune and Mem0, provides AI agents with persistent, company-specific context—enabling them to learn, adapt, and respond intelligently across multiple interactions. TrendMicro, one of the largest antivirus software companies in the world, developed the Trend’s Companion chatbot, so their customers can explore information through natural, conversational interactions From developer desks to the whole organization: Running Claude Cowork in Amazon Bedrock by Sofian Hamiti , Ayan Ray , and Antonio Rodriguez on 21 APR 2026 in Amazon Bedrock , Announcements , Generative AI Permalink Comments Share Today, we’re excited to announce Claude Cowork in Amazon Bedrock. You can now run Cowork and Claude Code Desktop through Amazon Bedrock, directly or using an LLM gateway. In this post, we walk through how Claude Cowork integrates with Amazon Bedrock and show an example of how knowledge workers use it in practice. End-to-end lineage with DVC and Amazon SageMaker AI MLflow apps by Manuwai Korber , Paolo Di Francesco , Nick McCarthy , and Sandeep Raveesh-Babu on 21 APR 2026 in Amazon SageMaker AI , Technical How-to Permalink Comments Share In this post, we show how to combine DVC (Data Version Control), Amazon SageMaker AI, and Amazon SageMaker AI MLflow Apps to build end-to-end ML model lineage. We walk through two deployable patterns — dataset-level lineage and record-level lineage — that you can run in your own AWS account using the companion notebooks. Accelerate Generative AI Inference on Amazon SageMaker AI with G7e Instances by Hazim Qudah , Dmitry Soldatkin , Venu Kanamatareddy , and Sanghwa Na on 20 APR 2026 in Amazon SageMaker , Amazon SageMaker AI , Artificial Intelligence , Technical How-to Permalink Comments Share Today, we are thrilled to announce the availability of G7e instances powered by NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs on Amazon SageMaker AI. You can provision nodes with 1, 2, 4, and 8 RTX PRO 6000 GPU instances, with each GPU providing 96 GB of GDDR7 memory. This launch provides the capability to use a single-node GPU, G7e.2xlarge instance to host powerful open source foundation models (FMs) like GPT-OSS-120B, Nemotron-3-Super-120B-A12B (NVFP4 variant), and Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, offering organizations a cost-effective and high-performing option. ToolSimulator: scalable tool testing for AI agents by Darren Wang , Smeet Dhakecha , Vinayak Arannil , and Xuan Qi on 20 APR 2026 in Artificial Intelligence , Strands Agents Permalink Comments Share You can use ToolSimulator, an LLM-powered tool simulation framework within Strands Evals, to thoroughly and safely test AI agents that rely on external tools, at scale. Instead of risking live API calls that expose personally identifiable information (PII), trigger unintended actions, or settling for static mocks that break with multi-turn workflows, you can use ToolSimulator’s large language model (LLM)-powered simulations to validate your agents. items.article_h1: AWS Security Agent and DevOps Agent Exit Preview, Enter General Availability
items.article_standfirst: Amazon has made its autonomous frontier agents — the AWS Security Agent for on-demand penetration testing and the AWS DevOps Agent — generally available, signaling a major step toward AI-driven cloud security and operations at scale.
article_body: Amazon Web Services has officially moved two of its flagship autonomous AI systems out of preview and into general availability. The AWS Security Agent, which provides on-demand penetration testing capabilities, and the AWS DevOps Agent are now broadly accessible to customers and partners, the company announced on 31 March 2026. Both agents fall under AWS's newly defined category of "frontier agents," a class of AI systems capable of working independently to achieve complex, long-running goals without continuous human oversight.

These frontier agents are designed to operate persistently for hours or even days, scaling concurrently across multiple tasks to handle security testing and cloud operations at a scale previously impractical for human-led teams. AWS previewed the capabilities as part of its re:Invent announcements, where early customers and partners reported that the Security Agent was able to comprehensively handle penetration testing workflows autonomously.

The release marks a deliberate shift in how AWS positions AI within enterprise cloud environments — not as a point-in-time assistant, but as a persistent, autonomous actor capable of carrying out extended security and operational workflows. Together, the two agents aim to change the way organizations both secure and operate their software on AWS infrastructure.
article_why_matters: Organizations can now automate penetration testing on demand, potentially reducing the cost and turnaround time of routine security assessments without relying solely on external consultants.

DevOps teams gain an AI agent capable of handling extended, multi-step cloud operations autonomously, freeing engineers from repetitive operational tasks and enabling them to focus on higher-value work.

The general availability signals that AWS views autonomous, long-running AI agents as a core product category — not an experimental feature — signaling a broader industry shift toward AI systems that act independently over hours or days rather than responding to single prompts.
article_source_note: This reporting is based on an official announcement published on the AWS Machine Learning Blog by AWS Vice President Swami Sivasubramanian on 31 March 2026. The post marks the general availability of two frontier agents announced at re:Invent. Details on pricing, regional availability, and specific capability limitations were not included in the available excerpt and have not been independently verified.

items.article_h1: Together AI Brings Inference, Agents, Voice AI, and Open Models to NVIDIA GTC 2026
items.article_standfirst: Together AI announced a slate of new launches across AI inference, agents, voice AI, and open models at NVIDIA GTC 2026, alongside technical sessions led by its research and engineering teams.
article_body: Together AI is appearing at NVIDIA GTC 2026 with a broad set of product and research announcements spanning four key areas: AI inference, agents, voice AI, and open models. The company says the showcase reflects its push to close the gap between raw GPU hardware and production-ready AI systems.

The announcement also spotlights Together AI's kernels team — the group behind well-known optimizations such as FlashAttention and the ThunderKittens library. According to the company, these researchers work to extract maximum performance from GPU hardware for real-world AI workloads.

Additional highlights on the blog include a post on 'AI Native Cloud' infrastructure, which the company frames as infrastructure purpose-built for model inference rather than adapted from legacy cloud architectures, and an introduction to Mamba-3, described as a state-space model (SSM) targeting inference efficiency with faster decode than Transformers.
article_why_matters: NVIDIA GTC is one of the most closely watched venues for AI infrastructure news; Together AI's presence signals it is competing directly with hyperscalers in inference and model serving.

The kernels team's FlashAttention work is already widely adopted in training pipelines — its focus on production inference optimization suggests Together AI is targeting cost and latency-sensitive deployment scenarios.

The breadth of the announcement — covering inference, agents, voice AI, and open models — indicates Together AI is positioning itself as a full-stack AI cloud provider rather than a narrow tooling vendor.
article_source_note: Information drawn from the Together AI blog landing page (together.ai/blog), last published on 23 April 2026. The page lists announcements and research highlights but does not yet include full technical details or release dates for individual products — readers should monitor the blog for follow-up posts with specifics on each launch.
